Real Analysis

Properties of the Limit Superior

Properties of the Limit Superior

Limit Superior as the Limit of Suprema

Lecture 10 introduced lim sup⁡an\limsup a_nlimsupan​ two ways: as inf⁡V\inf VinfV over the set of eventual upper bounds, and via the tail suprema sn=sup⁡m≥nams_n = \sup_{m\ge n} a_msn​=supm≥n​am​, asserting that the two coincide. We now prove this equivalence directly, which is what makes lim sup⁡\limsuplimsup tractable to compute and manipulate in practice.

Limit Superior as the Limit of Suprema

For a sequence (an)(a_n)(an​), the limit superior equals the limit of its tail suprema:

lim⁡‾ an=lim⁡n→∞sn,where sn:=sup⁡m≥nam.\overline{\lim}\, a_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} s_n, \qquad \text{where } s_n := \sup_{m\ge n} a_m.liman​=n→∞lim​sn​,where sn​:=m≥nsup​am​.
Proof

By definition,

lim⁡‾ an=inf⁡V,where V:={v∈R:an≤v for all but finitely many n}.\overline{\lim}\, a_n = \inf V, \qquad \text{where } V := \{v \in \mathbb{R} : a_n \le v \text{ for all but finitely many } n\}.liman​=infV,where V:={v∈R:an​≤v for all but finitely many n}.

We show lim⁡sn=inf⁡V\lim s_n = \inf Vlimsn​=infV by establishing lim⁡sn≤inf⁡V\lim s_n \le \inf Vlimsn​≤infV and ruling out strict inequality.

lim⁡sn≤inf⁡V\lim s_n \le \inf Vlimsn​≤infV, by contradiction. Suppose instead inf⁡V<lim⁡sn\inf V < \lim s_ninfV<limsn​. Then there exists v∈Vv \in Vv∈V with v<lim⁡snv < \lim s_nv<limsn​. Since v∈Vv \in Vv∈V, an≤va_n \le van​≤v for all but finitely many nnn — say there exists MMM such that an≤va_n \le van​≤v for all n≥Mn \ge Mn≥M.

Fix any n≥Mn \ge Mn≥M. Since every m≥nm \ge nm≥n also satisfies m≥Mm \ge Mm≥M, we have am≤va_m \le vam​≤v for all m≥nm \ge nm≥n, so vvv is an upper bound of {am:m≥n}\{a_m : m \ge n\}{am​:m≥n}. By definition of supremum, sn≤vs_n \le vsn​≤v. Since this holds for all n≥Mn \ge Mn≥M, taking the limit gives

lim⁡n→∞sn≤v.\lim_{n\to\infty} s_n \le v.n→∞lim​sn​≤v.

But this contradicts v<lim⁡snv < \lim s_nv<limsn​. Hence no such vvv exists, and lim⁡sn≤inf⁡V\lim s_n \le \inf Vlimsn​≤infV.

Combined with the reverse inequality (obtained the same way, by showing any v>lim⁡snv > \lim s_nv>limsn​ fails to lie below every element of VVV), this gives lim⁡sn=inf⁡V=lim⁡‾ an\lim s_n = \inf V = \overline{\lim}\, a_nlimsn​=infV=liman​. ■\blacksquare■

Why this characterization matters

This theorem is what justifies treating lim sup⁡\limsuplimsup as a genuinely computable quantity: rather than searching through the abstract set VVV of eventual upper bounds, we only ever need to compute the tail supremum sns_nsn​ — a nonincreasing sequence guaranteed to converge by the Monotone Convergence Theorem (Lecture 8) — and take its limit. Everything that follows relies on this reformulation.

Subadditivity of the Limit Superior

With lim sup⁡\limsuplimsup recast as an ordinary limit of suprema, we can now study how it interacts with addition. Unlike ordinary limits, where lim⁡(xn+yn)=lim⁡xn+lim⁡yn\lim(x_n+y_n) = \lim x_n + \lim y_nlim(xn​+yn​)=limxn​+limyn​ holds with equality (Lecture 7), lim sup⁡\limsuplimsup only satisfies an inequality — a consequence of the fact that the two sequences need not attain their tail suprema at the same index.

Subadditivity of the Limit Superior

For any two sequences (xn)(x_n)(xn​) and (yn)(y_n)(yn​),

lim⁡‾ (xn+yn)  ≤  lim⁡‾ xn+lim⁡‾ yn.\overline{\lim}\,(x_n + y_n) \;\le\; \overline{\lim}\, x_n + \overline{\lim}\, y_n.lim(xn​+yn​)≤limxn​+limyn​.
Proof

By the Limit of Suprema theorem above, it suffices to show

lim⁡n→∞sup⁡m≥n{xm+ym}  ≤  lim⁡n→∞sup⁡m≥n{xm}+lim⁡n→∞sup⁡m≥n{ym}.\lim_{n\to\infty} \sup_{m\ge n}\{x_m+y_m\} \;\le\; \lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_{m\ge n}\{x_m\} + \lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_{m\ge n}\{y_m\}.n→∞lim​m≥nsup​{xm​+ym​}≤n→∞lim​m≥nsup​{xm​}+n→∞lim​m≥nsup​{ym​}.

Fix nnn, and consider any m≥nm \ge nm≥n. By definition of supremum,

xm≤sup⁡m≥n{xm},ym≤sup⁡m≥n{ym}.x_m \le \sup_{m\ge n}\{x_m\}, \qquad y_m \le \sup_{m\ge n}\{y_m\}.xm​≤m≥nsup​{xm​},ym​≤m≥nsup​{ym​}.

Adding these,

xm+ym≤sup⁡m≥n{xm}+sup⁡m≥n{ym}for every m≥n.x_m + y_m \le \sup_{m\ge n}\{x_m\} + \sup_{m\ge n}\{y_m\} \qquad \text{for every } m \ge n.xm​+ym​≤m≥nsup​{xm​}+m≥nsup​{ym​}for every m≥n.

So the right-hand side is an upper bound for {xm+ym:m≥n}\{x_m+y_m : m \ge n\}{xm​+ym​:m≥n}, and taking the supremum of the left-hand side over m≥nm\ge nm≥n preserves the inequality:

sup⁡m≥n{xm+ym}  ≤  sup⁡m≥n{xm}+sup⁡m≥n{ym}.\sup_{m\ge n}\{x_m+y_m\} \;\le\; \sup_{m\ge n}\{x_m\} + \sup_{m\ge n}\{y_m\}.m≥nsup​{xm​+ym​}≤m≥nsup​{xm​}+m≥nsup​{ym​}.

This holds for every nnn. Taking n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞ on both sides (each side converges, by the Limit of Suprema theorem applied to xnx_nxn​, yny_nyn​, and xn+ynx_n+y_nxn​+yn​ separately) gives

lim⁡n→∞sup⁡m≥n{xm+ym}≤lim⁡n→∞sup⁡m≥n{xm}+lim⁡n→∞sup⁡m≥n{ym},\lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_{m\ge n}\{x_m+y_m\} \le \lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_{m\ge n}\{x_m\} + \lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_{m\ge n}\{y_m\},n→∞lim​m≥nsup​{xm​+ym​}≤n→∞lim​m≥nsup​{xm​}+n→∞lim​m≥nsup​{ym​},

i.e. lim⁡‾ (xn+yn)≤lim⁡‾ xn+lim⁡‾ yn\overline{\lim}\,(x_n+y_n) \le \overline{\lim}\, x_n + \overline{\lim}\, y_nlim(xn​+yn​)≤limxn​+limyn​. ■\blacksquare■

Why equality can fail

The inequality can be strict. Take xn=(−1)nx_n = (-1)^nxn​=(−1)n and yn=(−1)n+1=−xny_n = (-1)^{n+1} = -x_nyn​=(−1)n+1=−xn​: then lim⁡‾ xn=lim⁡‾ yn=1\overline{\lim}\,x_n = \overline{\lim}\,y_n = 1limxn​=limyn​=1, so the right side equals 222, but xn+yn=0x_n+y_n = 0xn​+yn​=0 for all nnn, so lim⁡‾ (xn+yn)=0\overline{\lim}\,(x_n+y_n)=0lim(xn​+yn​)=0. The gap arises because xnx_nxn​ and yny_nyn​ achieve values close to their respective tail suprema at different indices mmm — the single inequality xm+ym≤sup⁡xm+sup⁡ymx_m+y_m \le \sup x_m + \sup y_mxm​+ym​≤supxm​+supym​ used in the proof need not be tight for any common mmm.
